sections.heading: AI Problem Solving
sections.body: Many AI tasks can be framed as problem solving: given a starting situation, find a sequence of actions that leads to a desired outcome. This framing turns diverse real-world tasks — route planning, puzzle solving, game playing — into a common structure that shared algorithms can tackle.

sections.heading: State Space Search
sections.body: State space search is the process of systematically exploring the set of all possible states reachable from the initial state, using available actions, until a goal state is found. It's the general technique underlying most classical AI problem-solving methods.

sections.heading: Search Tree
sections.body: A search tree is built by expanding states: the root is the initial state, and each node's children are the states reachable by applying one available action. Note that the search tree can contain repeated states (revisiting the same situation via different paths), which is different from the underlying state space graph.

sections.heading: Goal State
sections.body: The goal state (or goal test) defines when the search is done — either a specific target state, or more generally a condition that any state can be checked against to determine if it satisfies the problem's objective.

sections.heading: Initial State
sections.body: The initial state is the starting configuration the agent begins in before taking any action — the root of the search tree and the reference point from which every solution path is measured.

sections.heading: Operators
sections.body: Operators (also called actions) are the legal moves available at each state, which transform one state into another. The full set of applicable operators at a given state defines that state's "branching factor" in the search tree.

sections.heading: Solution Path
sections.body: A solution path is the sequence of operators/actions that transforms the initial state into a goal state. Different search strategies may find different solution paths of different lengths and costs — which is why performance measures like optimality (finding the cheapest path) matter.

quiz.question: State space search systematically explores states until it finds:
options: Every possible operator, regardless of state

quiz.question: What does an "operator" represent in problem solving?
options: A legal action that transforms one state into another

quiz.question: A solution path is:
options: The sequence of actions transforming the initial state into a goal state
Only the goal state by itself
A description of the environment only
